summary: Gruber argues that simple pricing is a critical factor in consumer purchasing decisions, using Apple and Netflix as positive examples. He contrasts their straightforward approaches with the New York Times's newly announced digital subscription plan, which he finds both too expensive and too complex. The Times's three-tier system arbitrarily divides access by device screen size, and paradoxically charges less for print subscriptions that include full digital access than for digital-only plans. Gruber contends that since a digital NYT subscription is a want rather than a need, simple pricing is especially important. He believes a simpler, cheaper plan would attract more subscribers and generate more revenue, and worries the announced plan will damage the Times's brand.
key_points: Simple pricing removes friction from purchasing decisions; confusion drives people away since not paying is always simple
Companies that succeed with complex pricing tend to be monopolies or near-monopolies selling necessities, like cable companies and car dealers
The NYT's pricing paradoxically steers customers toward the legacy print business by charging less for print+digital than digital-only
Netflix exemplifies ideal digital pricing: one price, access from any device, easy to understand and cancel
Non-necessities especially require simple pricing because consumers can easily choose not to buy at all
money_quotes: If you make it easy for people to understand how much they're paying, and what they're paying for, it is more likely that they'll buy it.
The decision to purchase and the act of paying are part of the experience for any product or service, and should be designed accordingly.
Netflix: one price, access from any device. New York Times: three tiers, arbitrary division between devices based on screen size.

You pay less but get something that intuitively bears a significant real cost: hundreds of pounds of printed newspaper delivered to your home throughout the year. The pricing steers people toward the legacy business.
A digital NYT subscription is something few people will feel they need. Many people might want one, to one degree or another, but for few will it be a necessity.
I don't know that a simpler, lower-priced digital subscription plan would work for The Times, but I feel strongly that it would be more likely to work than what they've announced.
key_insight: Pricing is a design problem, and when a product isn't a necessity, confusing or expensive pricing will drive customers toward the simplest option of all: not paying.
